title: RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited v Nicole Hart Lathan (CL 17 of 2023) [2026] TCASC 19 (27 March 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF THE ACTION NO. CL 17/23
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
RBC ROYAL BANK (BAHAMAS) LIMITED Plaintiff
-and-
NICOLE HART LATHAN Defendant
Appearances: Ms Nayasha Hatmin of Wessex Fairchild for the Plaintiff
Mr George Missick of Geordins for the Defendants
Before: The Hon. Mr Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Hearing Date: 25th February 2026
Venue: Court 1, Supreme Court, Leeward Highway, Providenciales
DECISION
1. On 8 February 2023, the Plaintiff issued an originating summons seeking leave of the Court
under section 77 of the Registered Land Act (Cap 9.01) (‘the RLA’) to rely on variations of
section 72 and 75 of the RLA, as contained in clauses 5c. and 8.d of a charge granted by the
Defendant over parcel 60713/218/K10.
2. By way of background, the Plaintiff bank is the chargee of the subject parcel under a charge
dated 8 November 2013, and registered on 25 November 2013 (‘the Charge’). The Charge
was to secure advances made by the Plaintiff to the Defendant, totalling US$143,500.00.
The Defendant is the registered owner of the parcel and the chargor. The Defendant is in
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 1 of 16

default under the Charge, and the plaintiff is proceeding to enforce its security by way of
sale.
3. Notwithstanding that an Acknowledgement of Service was filed on 28 September 2023, no
request for the issue of a Notice of Hearing was made until 9 October 2025. The matter then
came before the Court on several occasions when adjournments were granted to allow the
Defendant time either to clear the balance or seek refinancing.
4. On 25 February 2026, it became apparent that the Defendant had been unsuccessful in her
endeavours, and the application progressed.
5. Section 75 of the RLA gives a statutory power of sale to a chargee in the event that the
chargor defaults in repayment of the monies due under the charge. The statutory power of
sale requires that the property concerned be sold by public auction, for which no application
to the Court is required; however, the power of sale only arises after the requirements of
sections 64(2) and 72 of the RLA have been complied with.1
6. Section 64(2) RLA provides:
(2) A date for the repayment of the money secured by a charge may be specified
in the charge instrument, and where no such date is specified or repayment is not
demanded by the chargee on the date specified the money shall be deemed to be
repayable three months after the service of a demand in writing by the chargee.
7. Section 72 RLA provides inter alia:
Chargee’s remedies
72. (1) If default is made in payment of the principal sum or of any interest or any
other periodical payment or of any part thereof, or in the performance or
observance of any agreement expressed or implied in any charge, and continues
for one month, the chargee may serve on the chargor notice in writing to pay the
money owing or to perform and observe the agreement, as the case may be.
(2) If the chargor does not comply, within three months of the date of service,
1 If a date for repayment is specified in the charge and a demand for payment is made on the date, section 64(2) is
of no effect; however, in practice, this is rarely the case.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 2 of 16

with a notice served on him under subsection (1), the chargee may—
…
(b) sell the charged property:
…
8. Section 77 of the RLA provides:
Variation of powers
77. The provisions of sections 70(2) and (3), 72, 73, 74 and 75 of this Ordinance
may in their application to a charge be varied or added to in the charge:
Provided that any such variation or addition shall not be acted upon, unless the
court, having regard to the proceedings and conduct of the parties and to the
circumstances of the case, so orders.
9. In the subject charge, sections 72 and 75 were varied as follows:
a. Clause 5.c. of the Charge provides:
“That section 72 of the Ordinance shall be varied and extended pursuant
to the provisions in that behalf contained in section 77 of the Ordinance so
that the powers of sale and of appointing a receiver thereby conferred and
the Bank’s right of foreclosure and any other powers and remedies of the
Bank whether hereunder or by Common Law, Ordinance or equity or
otherwise may be exercised by the Bank at any time immediately after a
demand for the repayment has been made to the Chargor or if at the time
of the exercise thereof any case shall have happened in which the Bank is
hereinbefore authorised to take possession of the Charged Property or any
default has occurred in respect of performance of any of the Chargor’s
covenants or obligations herein contained and it shall not be necessary to
give any such notice to the charger as is mentioned in section 72 of the
Ordinance.” (Emphasis added)
b. Clause 8.d. of the Charge provides:
“In accordance with the provisions of section 77 of the Ordinance, upon
the Bank obtaining an Order of the Court permitting this clause to be
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 3 of 16

enforced, Section 75 of the Ordinance shall be varied with respect to this
Charge so as to provide that the Bank in exercising its power of sale may
sell the Charged Property by private treaty instead of public auction if it
shall so determine in its sole and absolute discretion. For the purposes of
such application to the Court for consent to this variation, the Chargor
does hereby acknowledge that it shall be reasonable for the Bank to apply
to the Court to approve the Bank’s right to sell the Charged Property by
private treaty upon the Bank having been unable to sell the charged
property by way of a single public auction.” (Emphasis added)
10. In Meridian Mortgage Corporation Ltd -v- Jon Michael Hayes Shibley2 I referred to
the requirement for serving a notice pursuant to section 64(2) RLA in circumstances where
either the charge does not have a date for repayment or a demand for payment was not made
on the date specified. Most commonly, a section 64(2) notice, demanding payment of the
balance due, must be given before a power of sale arises where there is a default under the
terms of the charge, such as non-payment.
11. In this matter, as in Shibley, no section 64(2) notice has been served, and Ms Hatmin seeks
to rely on a purported service of a section 72 (default) notice, which provided for a period
of three months with which to comply, three months being the statutory demand period
provided by section 64(2). She can only do this if leave is given to rely on the variation of
section 72 such that no section 72 notice is required.3
12. The application is supported by an affidavit of Marcus Samuel, the then Country Manager
of the Plaintiff, sworn on 31 January 2023. In that affidavit, he states:
“The Plaintiff was unable to personally serve the Defendant [with] the Section 72
Notice. By way of alternative, pursuant to the Registered Land Ordinance, the
Section 72 Notice was advertised for three consecutive weeks in The Gazette Turks
and Caicos Islands (sic).
The Section 72 notice was also displayed in a prominent place on the property.”
2 (CL 110 of 2021) [2022] TCASC 37 (18 October 2022)
3 See Shibley; Paradise Manor Limited (in liquidation) W.M. Becker and M.L. Becker -v- Bank of Nova Scotia 1984-85
CILR 437.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 4 of 16

13. Exhibited to the affidavit are three photographs. The first is of an envelope affixed to a
chained and padlocked gate, which has stapled to it a document titled ‘Acknowledgment of
Service receipt, printed out on the letterhead of the Plaintiff’s attorneys. This document
reads:
“MATTER: NOTICE (Section 72(1) Registered Land Ordinance)
Your indebtedness to RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited”
14. It then has the Defendant’s name and US address and continues:
“I ACKNOWLEDGE that I have received a copy of the following documents:
DOCUMENT TYPE;
1. Demand Letter from Wessex Fairchild Attorneys dated 22nd July 2021
2. Demand Notice dated 22nd July 2021,”
15. There is then space provided for the insertion of the date served and received, to type or
print the name of the person served, and space for their signature. There is also space to
insert who served the document and the time and place of service. Those spaces are blank.
16. The second photograph is a wider shot of double gates chained and padlocked and showing
the above envelope affixed to the gate. The third photograph is a wider shot still, again
showing the envelope affixed to the gate and also showing a large skip or container beyond
the gates and a number of condominiums beyond. It is clear from the photographs, and was
confirmed by counsel, that the condominiums are not occupied and are derelict to the extent
that a substantial portion of the roof is missing, as are the windows. I was told that the
development had been significantly damaged by Hurricane Irma, which hit the Turks and
Caicos Islands in September 2017.
17. Section 151 RLA provides:
Service of notices
151. A notice under this Ordinance shall be deemed to have been served on or
given to any person––
(a) if served on him personally; or
(b) if served on an attorney holding a power of attorney whereunder such
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 5 of 16

attorney is authorised to accept such service; or
(c) if sent by registered post to him at his last known postal address in the
Islands or elsewhere and a receipt purporting to have been signed by him has
been received in return; or
(d) if service cannot be effected in one of the above-mentioned ways, by
displaying it in a prominent place on the land affected and by publishing it in
three consecutive issues of the Gazette.
18. The Defendant opposes the application on the basis that no power of sale has arisen due to
the fact that the Plaintiff has failed to show that the section 72 notice was lawfully served.
The following issues are taken:
a. No explanation is given as to why the Defendant could not be personally served.
b. The affidavit of Marcus Samuel does not say who served the notice.
c. There is no affidavit from whoever served the notice.
d. There is no indication when and where the notice was served.
e. The whereabouts of the gate to which the envelope is affixed is not identified, and
as such, the premises are not identified.
f. What was affixed to the gate is not a section 72 notice.
19. As such, Mr Missick submits that there is no evidence of proper service of the notice and in
consequence the Plaintiff has not discharged its statutory obligation and therefore no power
of sale has arisen.
20. Ms Hatmin, in reply, submits:
a. That the document described in paragraphs 13 to 15 above says what is in the
envelope.
b. That the failure of Mr Samuel is him not saying how he knew that the notice had
been served, not that it had not been served.
c. That by affixing the notice to the gate it was intended that it would come to the
attention of the Defendant.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 6 of 16

d. That the Defendant has not said that the notice did not come to her attention.
e. That what is being challenged is the strength of the evidence and that there is
sufficient evidence that the Court can find, on the balance of probabilities, that the
notice did come to the attention of the Defendant.
f. That when the proceedings were served, the Defendant entered an appearance by
returning an acknowledgement of service form.
21. Ms Hatmin was unable to say how the proceedings were served. This action was
commenced before the implementation of electronic filing, and this is a file that was
subsequently scanned onto the system; the Court’s hard copy file was not available to me.
There was nothing on the Court electronic file that suggested how the proceedings were
served.
22. Shortly after the hearing, on the same day, Ms Hatmin advised the Court that an application
for service out of the jurisdiction had been made and was granted by way of an order made
on 24 April 2023, and uploaded the relevant documents to the Court's electronic filing
system. That application was supported by an affidavit of Elise James (legal assistant with
the Plaintiff’s attorneys), sworn on 27 March 2023. In that affidavit, Ms James states:
“4. The Plaintiff understands that the Defendant no longer reside[s] in the
jurisdiction. The Defendant resides at … Raleigh, North Carolina, United States
of America….
5. In respect of service against the Defendant, it may be that personal service will
be possible at the residential address of the Defendant referred to in paragraph 4
above.”
23. Ms James does not explain the source of the Plaintiff’s knowledge, nor when this knowledge
was acquired, which given Mr Samuel’s evidence set out in paragraph 12 above, logically
must be after the date when the purported service of the section 72 notice was effected;
however, I note that the address for the Defendant given by Ms James is the same address
for the Defendant as appears in the Charge and on the purported section 72 notice.
24. No application for substituted service was made, and no affidavit of service explaining how
service was effected has been filed. No issue has been taken by the Defendant regarding
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 7 of 16

proper service, so I must assume that the proceedings were personally served on the
Defendant in the USA, as required by Ord. 10 r.5, Civil Rules 2000.
Discussion
25. As noted in paragraph 17 above, the RLA stipulates how notices under the RLA are to be
served or deemed to be served. The provision relied upon by the Plaintiff (section 151(d)
RLA) is, in my view, a provision that gives relief, or a form of substituted service, when
service cannot be effected in accordance with any of the foregoing subsections. It is,
therefore, incumbent on the Plaintiff to show why none of those foregoing methods of
service could be used.
26. No explanation is given as to why the Defendant was not personally served with the section
72 notice, save for the passing comment from Mr Samuel.4 I agree with Ramsay-Hale CJ in
Nancy Peters -v- Woodville Court Limited5 where in dealing with a challenge to service
on a company, she held:
“14. Before leaving the issue of service of the proceedings, I would just note that
the Defendant's application was brought under a Rule6 which specifically provides
that a failure to serve, in accordance with the statutory provisions for service, does
not render the proceedings a nullity which the defendant is entitled to set aside as
of right.
15. The purpose of the Rule as explained by the learned authors of the White Book,
"was to abolish the distinction between non-compliance with
procedural rules which renders proceedings a nullity and non-
compliance which merely renders proceedings irregular" with the
consequence that "a failure to comply with s 725 (1) of the
Companies Act 1985, which provides that a writ may be served on
a company by leaving it at or sending it by post to the company's
registered office ...does not, even on the basis that s 725(1) were
mandatory (a point being left open), constitute such an irregularity
4 See para. 12 above.
5 (CL 123/2017) [2018] TCASC 6 (20 April 2018)
6 Service of proceedings on a company under the Companies Ordinance.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 8 of 16

in the proceedings as to render them a nullity and entitle the
defendant to have them set aside ex debito justitiae as non-
compliance with section 725 (1) is a mere irregularity within 02 r1
(1)": note 2/1/3.
16. I concur with the view expressed in the same note that,
"02, r.1 should be applied liberally in order, so far as is reasonable
and proper to prevent injustice being caused to one party by
mindless adherence to technicalities [in] the rules of procedure."
17. In the last paragraph the following appears:
"Defective service of proceedings, however gross the defect, and
even a total failure to serve where the existence of the proceedings
is nevertheless known to the defendant is an irregularity which can
be cured by the Court by the exercise of discretion under 0 2 r 1."
18. Those observations are relevant to service of proceedings only.
19. With respect to the service of the Section 72 Demand Notice, it is common
ground that a chargee may only commence proceedings to sell property where a
demand notice has been issued pursuant to section 72 of the Registered Land
Ordinance ("RLO"), served in accordance with Section 151 of the RLO and the
money owing remains outstanding at the end of the time limited for payment. (My
emphasis)
27. Service of the section 72 RLA notice had to be effected in accordance with section 151
RLA. The provisions of section 151(d) cannot be utilised unless service under subsections
151(a) to (c) is not possible, and I do not find that the brief evidence from Mr Samuel is
sufficient to persuade me that service could not be effected under those foregoing
provisions, a fortiori, where it seems the originating process was personally served on the
Defendant and where the Defendant’s address has not changed since the Charge was
granted. I find that no notice under section 72 RLA was properly served, and as such, no
power of sale has arisen. Accordingly, this action is dismissed as it could not be commenced
until the power of sale had arisen.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 9 of 16

28. Before I leave the matter of service, in the event I am wrong on the above finding, I should
deal with the issues raised by Mr Missick in sub-paragraphs 18.b to 18.f above. I see much
force in his argument that even if service under section 151(d), in the circumstances of the
case, was permissible, I could not be sure on the balance of probabilities that the notice had
been duly served. I do not think that it was, for the following reasons:
a. The purported notice is affixed to a gate to a land parcel in which there are a number
of properties, this being a strata condominium development. The Defendant’s
property has the title number 60713/218/K10. This, in itself, indicates that there are
at least 10 property titles behind the gate.7
b. Even if I were to accept that the notice could be affixed to a gate that leads to a
number of properties, I could not be sure that this is the gate which leads to the
Defendant’s property, absent evidence of who affixed it and identified the property.
c. What has been affixed to the gate is not a section 72 RLA notice. It is an envelope
and an undated cover sheet. Whilst the coversheet lists what is purportedly in the
envelope, I have no evidence from anyone who prepared the envelope as to what
was in it.
d. Section 151(d) requires a copy of the notice to be displayed ‘in a prominent place
on the land affected’. I am not of the view that placing a notice in an opaque
envelope is ‘displaying it’. It cannot be seen at all.
29. Finally, I also make an observation which was not raised as an issue by counsel, that there
appears to be a condition precedent in this charge, that the ability to come to the Court to
seek leave to rely on the variation of section 75 to sell by private treaty, only arises after the
Plaintiff has been unable to sell the charged property by way of a single public auction.8
There has been no public auction. Mr Samuel states:
“The charged property has not been advertised for sale by public auction as it has
been judicially recogni[s]ed by the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court that
7 It occurs to me that, at best (and assuming this is the correct strata corporation land), the gate is on the common
property of the strata corporation and not the land charged. It is also possible that this is a separate parcel
altogether, and that the owners of the strata lots have a right of way over the land, but I am hypothesising.
8 See para. 9 above.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 10 of 16

distressed properties rarely sell by public auction in this jurisdiction. Moreover,
the Plaintiff’s attorneys have also advised that its past attempts to sell distressed
properties by public auction have been largely unsuccessful.”
30. In Bank of Butterfield (Cayman) Limited -v- Jervis and Jackson,9 in considering an
application by a chargee for leave to sell by private treaty following default in payment of
a secured loan,10 the court refused to grant leave as no public auction had been held before
the application was made. Smellie CJ held at paragraphs 5 to 7:
“As no order of the court is required for the chargee to exercise the power of sale
by public auction in keeping with s. 75 the court’s sanction is indeed required for
a sale by private treaty. It is the long-settled practice of this court that the variation
of the Law by way of the grant of leave to sell by private treaty instead of by public
auction will not be given until after a fair attempt by the chargee to sell by public
auction. The reason for this is obvious and important, and I will explain it as
follows.
Section 75 imposes a duty upon the chargee to act in good faith when exercising
the right to sell by public auction. One aspect of that duty requires the chargee to
seek to get the best price the market will yield (“the market price”). In order to do
so the chargee will obtain an independent valuation and seek to market the
property for sale by public auction at that valuation price or some other reserve
price that the chargee might set pursuant to section 75, but being mindful at all
times the such a reserve price can only be justified as being that which the market
will actually yield at the time.
Until that attempt to sell by public auction has been taken, the court can have no
way of knowing whether some other lower price to be fixed as the reserve price
for Sale by private treaty is a reasonable price.” (Emphasis added)
9 [2011 (1) CILR 54]. The reasons given for the decision were to be applied mutatis mutandis in Cayman National
Building Society -v- Wellington (Cause No.459 of 2010) in which Smellie CJ refused a similar application on the
same day.
10 Sections 64, 72, 75 and 77 of the Cayman Islands Registered Land Law are in identical terms as those in the RLA,
save that the RLA had the words ‘of this Ordinance’ added in section 77 when referring to the section numbers –
see para. 8 above.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 11 of 16

31. However, in Cayman National Building Society -v- Cranston11 Quin J noted12
submissions made by counsel for the plaintiff, as follows:
“Directing a chargee to instruct and pay attorneys to fix an auction, to draft terms
and conditions of sale, and to advertise and hold an auction will involve
considerable professional fees and significant time. This could well act to the
detriment of the chargee bank but more importantly will inevitably act the
detriment of the chargor, who will be settled with the professional costs for the
public auction, and further disadvantaged by delay and interest which will keep
accruing.”
32. In Cranston, counsel for the plaintiff suggested that the mischief which concerned the
learned Chief Justice in Jervis, was that the court was being asked to fix a reserve price
which might be less than the ‘market value’, as at paragraph 7, he continued:
“Yet the imprimatur of the court is what is ultimately sought by the chargee to
absolve it of its duty to obtain the best price when the court sanctions the sale at a
price less than that which the market with persistent effort over time might yield.”
(My emphasis)
33. It seems that in the Cayman Islands, a practice has developed that, before exercising a power
of sale, a chargee would first come to the court for a declaration that the chargor was in
default and seek the sanction of the court as to the price at which the property was to be
offered for sale. The headnote in Scotiabank & Trust (Cayman) Limited -v- Ebanks and
Gordon13 reads:
“A practice had developed whereby chargees seeking to exercise their power of
sale would apply to the court by originating summons for a declaration that the
chargeor was in default and present the evidence of a licensed appraiser or
chartered surveyor as to (i) the true current market value of the property; and (ii)
the market value if the property had to be sold within a short period of time. The
court could then sanction the listing of the property with a licensed real estate
11 [2011 (1) CILR 67].
12 At para. 26.
13 [2012 (1) CILR 401]
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 12 of 16

agent on the Multiple Listing Service at a list price at least as high as (and often
higher than) the true market value and, if time passed without a sale, could later
authori[s]e the chargee to list the property at a lower price.”
34. It is not common practice in the Turks and Caicos Islands to seek sanction from, or for the
Court to fix, a reserve price for which a property is to be offered at auction or for the price
at which it is to be offered for sale by private treaty. As was argued in Cranston, a chargee
remains under an obligation to obtain the best possible price for a property, in compliance
with its section 72 statutory obligation to ‘act in good faith and have regard to the interests
of the chargor’, and if it does not, then the chargor will have a remedy against it.
35. In Barclays Bank PLC -v- Henry and Another14 whilst the application was for leave to
sell by private treaty, to which there was no dispute and both parties agreed that was the
preferred way forward, the issue was the sale price. Ground CJ considered:
a. whether the Court has power on such an application to approve a specific
transaction, or a specific price; and
b. if it does, whether it is appropriate on the facts of this case to approve the proposed
sale price.
36. Ground CJ did not make any specific finding that the Court did or did not have the power,
but declined to sanction the proposed transaction and referred to the duties imposed on a
chargee when exercising its power of sale. He made the following observation with respect
to section 77:
“I think it is important to note that this provision does not confer upon the Court
the power to alter the statute, nor does it confer in terms the power to approve the
sale, or a sale at a particular price. What it empowers the Court to do is to give
its sanction to the agreement between the parties overriding the statutory
provisions. In other words, if the contract between the parties has been embodied
in the charge, and that permits sale by some means other than public auction, then
that can prevail over the statutory restriction but only if the court decrees that it
may be acted upon.
14 (CL 80 of 1999) [1999] TCASC 3 (15 December 1999).
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 13 of 16

37. In Cranston, Quin J observed that section 77 gives the court a wide discretion and that it
does not state that the variation in the charge can only be acted upon after there has been an
unsuccessful auction. He agreed with counsel’s submission that the judgment in Jervis
“could unnecessarily restrict the court’s discretion and is not what the legislation intended”.
38. It appears that Smellie CJ was not referred to Bank of Nova Scotia Trust Co. (Cayman)
Ltd. -v- Bodden.15 Quin J says that he was provided with a full written judgment of Moody
J in that matter, by the consulting editor of The Cayman Islands Law Reports and makes
reference that the same argument was before the court, namely, that the application for leave
to sell by private treaty could only be brought after exhausting the power of sale by public
auction. He references16 the ratio of the judgment extracted by the learned editors of The
Cayman Islands Law Reports,17 which reads:
“As the power of sale given by the mortgage itself was effectively one to sell either
by public auction or private contract, it was not necessary to offer the property for
sale by public auction first and the court would order that the mortgagee be at
liberty to sell by private contract immediately.”
39. In my view the Court has the power to grant leave to sell by private treaty, absent an
unsuccessful public auction first being held, which the Court has done on numerous
occasions, albeit the reasoning not having been expounded as far as I am aware, and I adopt
the views of Ground CJ, as mooted by Mr Samuel;18 however, I do not think it would be
right to grant leave to sell by private treaty in circumstances where the Charge specifically
requires a public auction to have taken place first, as in this case.
40. I have set out in paragraph 9 above the provision of the Charge which varies section 75 of
the RLA. It expressly provides:
“For the purposes of such application to the Court for consent to this variation,
15 19 August 1997 - Unreported.
16 At para. 35.
17 (1952-79 CILR N-8).
18 Ground CJ in Barclays Bank PLC -v- Henry and Another (CL 80 of 1999) [1999] TCASC 3, where he says: “It is
perhaps unfortunate, in this day and age and in the context of a small island jurisdiction, that the Ordinance still
insists upon sale by public auction… I can well understand, and accept that a sale by public auction is often not
feasible and may also be in the interest of neither party because it tends to produce a sale at an undervalue, if a
sale can be achieved at all.”
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 14 of 16

the Chargor does hereby acknowledge that it shall be reasonable for the Bank to
apply to the Court to approve the Bank’s right to sell the Charged Property by
private treaty upon the Bank having been unable to sell the charged property by
way of a single public auction.” (Emphasis added)
41. The Plaintiff must be taken to have been aware of this provision, although it has not been
addressed by Mr Samuel or counsel. I would make the observation that it seems that the
applicable principle would be that laid down by the Court of Appeal in Paradise Manor
Limited (in liquidation) W.M. Becker and M.L. Becker -v- Bank of Nova Scotia19 that,
as per Zacca P.,20 where variations had been agreed “in their contractual agreements, it
cannot be said that it would be unfair or unreasonable, having regard to all the
circumstances, for the learned trial judge to have considered a variation of s.72 under the
summons before him.” And as per Kerr JA,21 “[t]he Agreements were between the parties
at arm’s length with legal advice at their beck and call. The applicant was asking for no
more than what was agreed. It could never be urged with reason and good sense that a
party is taken by surprise, when the other party in whose favour stipulations are made, asks
that effect be given to such stipulations.”22
42. Accordingly, this application has been brought prematurely. The Plaintiff could not come
to the Court until it had held a public auction which did not produce a buyer.
43. In conclusion, I thank counsel for their assistance in this matter and acknowledge that this
was a matter Ms Hatmin inherited and that she was not responsible for the errors and
omissions I have found.
Disposition
44. The Originating Summons dated 7 February 2023 and issued on 8 February 2023 is
dismissed. The Plaintiff shall pay the Defendant’s costs of and occasioned by the application
to be taxed on the standard basis if not agreed and I record the view of the Court that the
costs of this application should not be visited upon the chargor by reliance on the terms of
19 1984-85 CILR 437.
20 At [452] 15.
21 At [465] 15.
22 See also Cranston at paras. 43 and 44.
CL - 17/23 – RBC Royal Bank (Bahamas) Limited -v- Nicole Hart Lathan
Page 15 of 16

the Charge.
27th March 2026
The Hon. Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Judge of the Supreme Court
